claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age(s) of the child(ren) you're enquiring about, and their name(s) so I can keep track if there's more than one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're welcome, I'm happy to help you with your Child Benefit claim for Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old, he falls into the **16 to 19 age category**. For children in this age group, there are some additional requirements to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For 17-year-old Alex, I need to understand his current situation with education or training:

1. **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (This includes school, college, A-levels, T-levels, apprenticeships, or similar qualifying courses)

2. **Has Alex recently left education or training?** (Within the last few months)

3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Which of these best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in college doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - Alex is in college doing A-levels. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **A-levels are on the approved list** of qualifications for Child Benefit purposes. I just need to confirm a couple more details about Alex's studies:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/tuition)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer**, or has Alex agreed to work for an employer after finishing the course as a condition of doing it?

For most college A-level students, the answer would be "yes" to full-time and "no" to employer funding - but please confirm so I can continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's studying full-time at college. And no, it's not funded by an employer - it's just regular college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming that. A-levels at college, full-time, with no employer funding - that all qualifies as **approved education**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about any paid work Alex might have:

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week in paid employment?** (Or does he not have a paid job at all?)

This includes any part-time job he might have alongside his studies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, he does work. He has a full-time job at the supermarket alongside his studies - he works more than 24 hours a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. Unfortunately, this is an important factor in the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to share this news, but based on the information you've provided, **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary for Alex

| Child | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | ❌ Not Eligible | Working 24+ hours per week |

### Why is Alex not eligible?

Although Alex ticks many of the right boxes:
- ✅ Aged 17 (within 16-19 range)
- ✅ In approved education (A-levels)
- ✅ Full-time study (12+ hours per week)
- ✅ Not employer-funded

The issue is:
- ❌ **He works 24 or more hours per week** in paid employment

Under Child Benefit rules, young people aged 16 or over who work **24 hours or more per week** are not eligible, even if they're still in education.

---

### What you could do:

1. **If Alex reduces his working hours** to less than 24 hours per week while continuing his studies, you may become eligible to claim again.

2. **Keep this in mind for the future** - if his work situation changes, you can make a new claim.

If you have any other questions or if Alex's circumstances change, please don't hesitate to ask. I'm sorry the outcome wasn't what you were hoping for.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is enrolled in approved education (A-levels at college, full-time, not employer-funded). However, he is also working 24 or more hours per week in paid employment at a supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are in approved education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex because, although Alex meets the education requirements (full-time A-levels), he exceeds the maximum permitted working hours of 24 hours per week.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is enrolled in approved education (A-levels at college, full-time, not employer-funded). However, he is also working 24 or more hours per week in paid employment at a supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are in approved education.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex because, although Alex meets the education requirements (full-time A-levels), he exceeds the maximum permitted working hours of 24 hours per week.
